title: Lesson 359
transcript: We have a very interesting topic to discuss here, which likely you won't see many questions about on the exam, but is going to become an ever greater and more important part of security very quickly in the next few years here. And that's machine learning and artificial intelligence based tools. So, first of all, you know, like maybe let's start with a definition of, you know, what's the kind of the basic idea behind these ML and AI tools? One of the fundamental... And why is this going to be so important to security? One of the things, especially in security tools, one of the things that we've been trying to do for many, many years is explicitly program our systems to do certain things. So we just talked about, for instance, different types of anti-malware systems. And we talked about how there are signature-based and... John, what's the other one? Anomaly-based. No, no, not anomaly. You know, in anti-malware systems. Heuristics. I heard it from Lou yelling downstairs. Perfect. Heuristics. There you go. Thank you. Really, I know what I'm talking about. Maybe. So heuristic based systems. The idea behind heuristic based systems is they can look for suspicious software without being explicitly told, you know, this is, without using signatures. And as I mentioned, heuristics-based systems use either static or dynamic scanning. Now, heuristic systems are a brilliant idea, but they just aren't really that prevalent today because it's exceptionally difficult to explicitly program a heuristic system to look for and figure out what is suspicious code. And there's a lot, this is true for a lot of problems that we have in computing. It's really hard to explicitly program computer systems and to do certain things. Let me give you a kind of another interesting example, and that's walking, right? For years, people have tried to build robots that can walk, especially bipedal robots. If you want to look into this, right, go and look up Boston Dynamics. They've been at this for close to a couple decades now. And some of their earlier prototypes were pretty primitive. It's really, and here's the challenge. It's really, really hard to explicitly program a robot how to walk. you know how to lift your feet how much to move it forward when to bring your foot down you can get a very simple sort of like gate going but as soon as the ground changes a little bit there's a little bit of ice on the ground incline anything like that and it just totally throws the system off And so for like decades, researchers were trying to figure out how to explicitly program computers to walk and they weren't really making a huge amount of success. That has changed rapidly though. The core idea behind a lot of these machine learning and AI algorithms is that instead of having to explicitly program a system how to do something, you get the system to learn how to do something by itself. And that kind of mimics a lot of biological systems, right? Like, as human beings, no one explicitly tells us how to walk, right? We just figure it out by falling over a lot. I've got a couple of kids. It was amazing watching them learn to walk and start to toddle around. It was such a cool thing to see and experience. And that's kind of the idea behind these machine learning and AI systems is... if you can essentially train them to do something without having to explicitly say, do this, then do that. Often they're trained on very large data sets. So you give them a whole bunch of kind of learning data and they'll develop an algorithm that will solve certain problems. And what's often fascinating about this is once these algorithms have been trained, the researchers that kind of built these systems, they don't understand the algorithms. Like they've learned something quite unique and often undecipherable on their own. And these techniques are now, of course, being heavily applied in the security space. I mean, the big cloud providers especially, other companies as well, but the big cloud providers, they're investing massively. Like Google's a great example. Google employs a vast number of AI researchers, and quite a few of them are working in the security space, trying to figure out essentially how to make our computer systems more resilient, have more like an automatic immune system and learn to identify problems on their own and potentially even start to fix these problems. And so here's some of the kind of major upcoming applications of AI that we're gonna see, I would bet, more and more of in the next few years. One of the big one is in threat detection and classification. So instead of having to explicitly program your SIM system to look for these types of attacks, these systems should be able to start to train themselves. Network risk scoring, helping to sort of suss out the impact and the severity of certain types of risks. Automating a lot of certain routines. You know, there's a bunch of different applications of this. Again, don't expect any specific questions about this on the exam because this is a very early nascent space, but it is rapidly developing. And I think we'll see some really amazing innovations in this space in the next few years. It'll be quite exciting to watch. That is until, you know, all these machine learning algorithms put us out of a job. Not looking forward to that. But, you know, hopefully by the time, that won't happen until I'm retired. Same for you. Not in our lifetimes. Not in our lifetimes, says John.
